_note: US market DEMO data (verified:false) — verify against real labels before indexing. No 'test' object = 'label-based review, hands-on test pending'.

foods.name: The Farmer's Dog — Turkey Recipe
foods.verdict: The benchmark US fresh diet: human-grade named ingredients, board-certified nutritionists on staff, AAFCO-complete recipes and portioning per dog. The honest caveat is price — cost per 1,000 kcal runs several times a quality kibble, and 'fresh extends lifespan' remains unproven.
pros: Named, human-grade ingredients; turkey recipe is chicken-free
Pre-portioned to your dog's caloric needs
cons: Cost per 1,000 kcal several times higher than kibble
Requires fridge/freezer logistics
Health-outcome superiority over kibble is not proven

faq.q: Is The Farmer's Dog worth the price?
faq.a: Nutritionally it is excellent and palatability is outstanding — but a top-tier kibble meets the same AAFCO standards at a fraction of the cost per 1,000 kcal. It is worth it for fussy eaters, small dogs (smaller absolute cost) and owners who value the convenience.

foods.name: Orijen Original
foods.verdict: The highest-meat mainstream kibble in the US: 85% animal ingredients, all named, with fresh and raw inclusions. Legumes appear mid-list (DCM context applies for predisposed breeds). Since the Mars acquisition the manufacturer pillar benefits from corporate QC infrastructure.
pros: 42% protein DM from named animal sources
cons: Legumes present in the recipe (DCM context)
High energy density — easy to overfeed without measuring
Premium price

faq.q: Is Orijen safe given the FDA DCM investigation?
faq.a: The FDA never established causality and stopped updates in 2022. Orijen contains legumes mid-recipe; for Dobermanns, Great Danes, Boxers and Goldens we suggest discussing grain-free choices with your veterinarian.

foods.name: Open Farm Homestead Turkey
foods.verdict: The transparency leader: every ingredient lot traceable on their website, certified-humane sourcing, named turkey first. Recipe quality is strong though not extreme — what earns the score is a manufacturer pillar most brands cannot match.
cons: Legumes in the recipe (DCM context)
Premium price for moderate protein level

faq.q: What makes Open Farm different from other premium kibbles?
faq.a: Traceability: you can enter the lot code and see the source of every ingredient. In our algorithm that means a near-maximal manufacturer-credibility pillar — the part of food quality you cannot read off a label.

foods.name: Ollie — Fresh Beef Recipe
foods.verdict: A strong fresh-food alternative to The Farmer's Dog: named beef recipe, vet-nutritionist formulation, flexible plans. Slightly behind on transparency of feeding-trial evidence; identical economics caveat — fresh costs several times kibble per 1,000 kcal.
cons: High cost per 1,000 kcal
Higher fat — not suitable after pancreatitis
Less published detail than category leader

faq.a: Both are nutritionally complete fresh diets. Farmer's Dog publishes more about its nutrition team and processes (higher manufacturer pillar); Ollie's mixed fresh+baked plans cut the monthly cost meaningfully.

foods.name: Wellness CORE Original
foods.verdict: A solid high-protein mainstream option: named turkey and chicken meals, added taurine and joint support claims at sensible levels. Legumes feature in the grain-free recipe — the brand also offers grain-inclusive CORE lines worth considering for predisposed breeds.
pros: 37.8% protein DM at a mid-premium price
cons: Legumes high in the grain-free recipe (DCM context)
Contains chicken
Glucosamine claims — evidence for dietary doses is weak

faq.q: Does the added glucosamine in CORE help joints?
faq.a: Meta-analyses show no significant clinical effect for glucosamine/chondroitin in dogs, and dietary doses are lower still. We treat it as neutral — neither a benefit nor a flaw. For joints, look for declared EPA/DHA instead.

foods.name: Purina Pro Plan Complete Essentials Adult
foods.verdict: The inverse of boutique brands: an ingredient list with by-products and cereals — but feeding trials, 500+ scientists, owned plants and the most rigorous QC in the industry. The food most often fed by US veterinarians to their own dogs. Pillar C carries the score.
pros: Industry-leading manufacturer infrastructure and recalls record
cons: By-products and cereals lead parts of the recipe (named, but mid-quality)
Contains chicken
Artificial preservation in some lines

faq.q: Why do vets recommend Pro Plan if the ingredients look mediocre?
faq.a: Because ingredient lists do not measure digestibility or safety. Purina runs feeding trials, employs boarded nutritionists and controls its plants — the evidence-backed parts of food quality. Our algorithm shows both truths: modest pillar A, near-maximal pillar C.

foods.name: Taste of the Wild High Prairie
foods.verdict: America's best-selling grain-free food: named bison and venison flavoring a chicken-meal base, at a budget-premium price. Two caveats: legumes feature prominently (it appeared frequently in FDA DCM reports — association, not causation), and Diamond's recall history weighs on the manufacturer pillar.
pros: Strong cost per 1,000 kcal for the protein level
cons: Legume-heavy recipe; frequent in FDA DCM case reports (association only)
Manufacturer recall history (2012 Salmonella)
Marketing implies more bison than the recipe contains

faq.q: Was Taste of the Wild linked to DCM?
faq.a: It appeared among the most-named brands in FDA case reports (53 reports through 2019). The FDA never established causation and notes reporting bias. For predisposed breeds, this is the category where we suggest extra caution and a vet conversation.

foods.name: Hill's Prescription Diet k/d Kidney Care
foods.verdict: A therapeutic renal diet — judged in its own category, where restricted phosphorus and moderated protein are clinical goals, not flaws (IRIS guidelines). Decades of feeding trials and published research behind it. Use strictly under veterinary supervision.
pros: Feeding trials and published renal research
High palatability for a therapeutic diet
cons: Not for healthy dogs — low protein is intentional and unsuitable
Premium price
Requires veterinary recommendation and monitoring

faq.q: Can I feed k/d to my healthy dog?
faq.a: No. Restricted phosphorus and moderated protein serve dogs with diagnosed kidney disease (IRIS staging). For a healthy dog this profile is unnecessarily low in protein. Therapeutic diets belong with a diagnosis and veterinary monitoring.
